column2: The Bedford-Pine Urban Renewal Area (Project Georgia A-2-1) is a 278.7-acre Neighborhood Development Program in eastern Atlanta, about 1.25 miles from City Hall, that has already produced a civic center, an elementary school, public housing, and hospital expansion since 1968. The remaining action is the disposition of a 78-acre Major Redevelopment Area to Park Central Communities, Inc. for roughly $11 million under a Comprehensive Development Plan. That plan calls for about 3,000 dwelling units (two-thirds owner-occupied), about 2.6 million square feet of office space, 250,000 square feet of …

column2: Bedford-Pine is an older inner-city Atlanta neighborhood that is predominantly Black and low-income, with median family income around $5,000, about 39% of residents below the poverty line, and a high share of elderly persons and renters in overcrowded, deteriorated housing. The community has higher-than-average crime rates, low school achievement scores at local elementary and middle schools, and heavy reliance on Grady Memorial Hospital for health care. Roughly 1,871 families were scheduled for relocation, and remaining residents include low-income households, a few homeowners, and small min…

The statement evaluates a do-nothing option, alternative sites, alternative land uses (predominantly commercial or predominantly residential), and several design alternatives, ultimately selecting Design Alternative C as the recommended action. It also considers variations in unit mix, density, and architectural prototypes, options for closing or relocating Felton Drive, an optional people-mover link to downtown, and a site-wide total energy system that was dropped for cost reasons. Additional alternatives address middle school capacity, relocation strategies (including on-site temporary hous…

Beneficial impacts include replacing slum and blighted conditions with new housing close to jobs and downtown, improved aesthetics, preservation of mature trees and topography, and stronger fire protection and employment opportunities. Adverse impacts include construction-period dust, noise, erosion, and traffic disruption; long-term increases in traffic, air pollution (with CO standards already exceeded in the area), and noise along Piedmont Avenue and the Stone Mountain Freeway corridor; about 1.58 million gallons per day of new sewage and large stormwater flows; and shortfalls in open spac…

A lawsuit (Mildred West v. Atlanta Housing Authority) halted land conveyance until this EIS was completed, and a related case (Freeman v. Housing Authority) alleged NEPA non-compliance, while community pressure also produced a moratorium on displacement and a one-for-one replacement housing agreement. Many residents have expressed strong attachment to the neighborhood, reluctance to relocate, concern that reduced low-rent public housing breaks earlier promises, and frustration over inadequate shopping, services, and police response.
